evidence: `goseg/protocol/actionids/actionids.go` defines canonical namespace/action strings, while `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` re-wraps them into a second exported token layer and also constructs contract IDs from those values.

`goseg/protocol/conformance/fixtures_test.go` validates fixtures against `groundseg/protocol/actions` symbols rather than directly against the `actionids` source, indicating action identity is mediated through multiple modules instead of one boundary.

`goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` keeps parallel exported aliases (`UploadOpenEndpointAction` and `UploadActionOpenEndpoint`, `UploadResetAction` and `UploadActionReset`), and `goseg/protocol/contracts/family_specs_test.go` still exercises alias names, showing compatibility shim paths are active rather than sunset.

`goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go` enforces upload action support locally (`len(actions) == 2`) and by local map checks, but does not anchor to protocol contract catalog IDs, so cross-module drift can pass in one module while failing in another.

Holistic structure shows large flat directories with mixed concerns: goseg/config/ (41 files), goseg/shipworkflow/ (49 files), and goseg/system/ (36 files), which exceeds the prompt’s >10 mixed-concern overload signal.

goseg/system/disk_shims_test.go contains non-test exported compatibility functions (e.g., ListHardDisks, CreateGroundSegFilesystem) despite `_test.go` placement, while adjacent files are true tests (disk_health_test.go, disk_storage_test.go).

goseg/config/bootstrap.go owns startup/bootstrap behavior plus runtime environment detection (getBasePath/getArchitecture/setDebugMode), while goseg/config/config.go owns runtime context model/mutators; the runtime-context concern is split across files with different responsibilities in the same overloaded folder.

goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle.go defines broadly reusable polling/config persistence helpers (PollWithTimeout, PersistUrbitConfig) inside a package already containing many workflow-specific files, increasing boundary ambiguity.

Runtime dependency/config bags are broad and mixed across domains: `AccessPointRuntime` combines AP config fields and many behavior seams, then `normalizeAccessPointRuntime` fills defaults for both (`goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint.go`), while `wifiRuntime` repeats the same mixed pattern (`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go`).

Protocol action API has repeated namespace-specific wrappers over the same core behavior (`ParseUploadAction`/`ParseC2CAction`, `SupportedUploadActions`/`SupportedC2CActions`, metadata/deprecation helpers) in `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go`.

Config mutation flow is section-by-section boilerplate duplicated in multiple layers: `SysConfig.Update*Config` methods repeat the same nil-check + mutate pattern (`goseg/structs/configs.go`) while `config_update_schema.go` maintains parallel section patch structs and field wiring (`goseg/config/config_update_schema.go`).

Tests show recurring per-package seam-reset harnesses and global-state orchestration (`resetWifiSeamsForTest`, `resetLeakStateForTest`, `resetRouterGlobalsForTest`) across multiple files, indicating production state/control surfaces are hard to isolate cleanly.

findings.summary: Action identity is split across multiple modules, creating a hub-like coupling pattern with no single authoritative boundary.
evidence: `actionids` owns raw tokens, `contracts` exports converted action/namespace constants and contract IDs, and conformance tests bind through `protocol/actions`, so identity validity depends on synchronization across at least three modules.

Upload tests check local supported actions and local contract maps, while protocol conformance checks fixture validity through a different symbol path; this is cross-module coordination by convention rather than one owned API surface.
findings.suggestion: Define one authoritative protocol action registry package (tokens + binding metadata) and make `contracts`, `actions`, conformance fixtures, and upload action parsing consume that registry directly. Remove parallel identity derivations and replace ad-hoc cross-module checks with one shared conformance entrypoint.

findings.summary: Back-compat alias constants keep duplicate contract identity paths alive, blurring dependency boundaries.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` exports both canonical IDs and legacy aliases (`UploadActionOpenEndpoint`, `UploadActionReset`) that point to the same values.
`family_specs_test.go` still references alias-style IDs, demonstrating the compatibility path is still part of active tests instead of being isolated behind migration boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a bounded deprecation plan: keep one canonical exported name per contract ID, migrate tests/callers to canonical symbols, and move any remaining compatibility aliases behind an explicitly versioned compatibility module with an expiry policy.

findings.summary: Production disk shim API is placed in a `_test.go` file, obscuring module ownership.
evidence: goseg/system/disk_shims_test.go defines non-test wrappers like ListHardDisks, CreateGroundSegFilesystem, SmartCheckAllDrives, and fstab helpers.
Adjacent files in the same cluster are actual tests and call those wrappers, reinforcing an implicit production surface hidden behind test-file naming.
This placement blurs whether disk orchestration belongs to goseg/system/, goseg/system/disk/, or goseg/system/storage/.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) move shim declarations out of `_test.go` into `goseg/system/disk_shims.go` (or `goseg/system/disk_bridge.go`) to make ownership explicit; (2) keep only tests in `disk_shims_test.go`, updating any package-level helper setup there; (3) if wrappers are only compatibility bridges, place them in `goseg/system/storage/bridge.go` and expose minimal forwarding API from `goseg/system`; (4) run `go test ./goseg/system/...` and `go test ./...` to validate imports and package boundaries after moves.

findings.summary: Runtime context concern is split across bootstrap and config state files in an overloaded package.
evidence: bootstrap.go defines environment/runtime detection functions (getBasePath/getArchitecture/setDebugMode) and initialization flow.
config.go defines RuntimeContext model and mutators (SetBasePath/SetArchitecture/SetDebugMode/SetDockerDir), creating one concern spread across unrelated file roles.
bootstrap_test.go targets runtime/env helpers that are not strictly bootstrap orchestration behavior, confirming mixed ownership.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/config/runtimecontext/` (or `goseg/config/runtime/`) for runtime context model + env/default resolvers; (2) move RuntimeContext types/mutators from `config.go` first, then move getBasePath/getArchitecture/setDebugMode and their tests from `bootstrap.go`/`bootstrap_test.go`; (3) keep `bootstrap.go` as orchestration-only initialization entrypoint calling the runtimecontext package; (4) run `go test ./goseg/config/...` and `go test ./...` to verify import updates and startup behavior.

findings.summary: Generic lifecycle helpers remain inside an already overloaded `shipworkflow` package.
evidence: lifecycle.go includes generic helpers (PollWithTimeout, PersistUrbitConfig contract wrapper) alongside ship-stop logic.
shipworkflow package profile is large (49 files), indicating broad responsibilities where helper placement has high navigability cost.
doc.go describes broad workflow coordination scope, while lifecycle.go mixes domain flow and generic utility behavior.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) extract generic polling/config-persist helpers from `shipworkflow/lifecycle.go` into `goseg/lifecycle/` or `goseg/internal/workflow/` as reusable primitives; (2) keep only ship-specific orchestration (WaitForUrbitStop*) in `shipworkflow`; (3) move/adjust tests so utility tests live with the extracted package and shipworkflow tests cover workflow semantics only; (4) run `go test ./goseg/shipworkflow/... ./goseg/lifecycle/...` (or target package) and then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Protocol action identity is owned by three layers, increasing drift risk across contracts and fixtures.
evidence: `actionids.go` defines canonical strings (`NamespaceUpload`, `NamespaceC2C`, `UploadOpenEndpoint`, `C2CConnect`).
`protocol_contracts.go` wraps those values into separate typed constants and derives contract IDs from them.
`fixtures_test.go` checks canonical fixture coverage against `protocol/actions` constants, not `actionids`, so canonical mapping is not validated from one single owner package.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate action vocabulary ownership into one package (for example `goseg/protocol/actionspec`) that exports namespace/action tokens and binding helpers. Then: 1) move constants from `actionids` into that package, 2) update `protocol_contracts.go` to consume only that API, 3) update conformance fixtures/tests to validate against the same package, 4) run `go test ./goseg/protocol/contracts ./goseg/protocol/conformance ./goseg/uploadsvc/...` and `go test ./...` to confirm boundary alignment.

findings.summary: Governance compatibility policy is duplicated per family without a shared enforcement boundary.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` hard-codes per-entry governance fields (`OwnerModule`, `Stability`, `SinceVersion`) for protocol actions.
`startram_contracts.go` repeats the same governance field pattern independently for StarTram contracts.

`README.md` defines backend quality-gate policy at top-level, but runtime governance compatibility rules are encoded as repeated literals in each family file rather than a single architecture contract.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared governance policy constructor (for example `newGovernedContract(owner, since, opts...)`) in contracts infrastructure and require family specs to use it. Stage rollout: 1) add constructor + validation hooks in shared contracts code, 2) migrate `protocol_contracts.go`, 3) migrate `startram_contracts.go`, 4) add a regression test asserting identical policy invariants across families, 5) run `go test ./goseg/protocol/contracts ./...`.

findings.summary: The contracts package mixes protocol-action and StarTram-error domains in one folder boundary.
evidence: `goseg/protocol/contracts/` has `file_count: 11` in `holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles`, crossing the flat-directory overload heuristic.
Within that folder, `protocol_contracts.go` defines action-bound websocket protocol IDs, while `startram_contracts.go` defines StarTram API error contracts; these are different ownership domains.
Root docs (`README.md`) separate backend quality/gov concerns by subsystem, but the runtime contract code layout does not reflect those subsystem boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) create target folders `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol/`, `goseg/protocol/contracts/startram/`, and `goseg/protocol/contracts/internal/family/` for shared validators; 2) move `protocol_contracts.go` (+tests) to `contracts/protocol/`; 3) move `startram_contracts.go` (+tests) to `contracts/startram/`; 4) move shared family-spec helpers to `contracts/internal/family/`; 5) run import updates with `goimports -w goseg/protocol/contracts/... goseg/protocol/conformance/...`; 6) validate with `go test ./goseg/protocol/contracts/... ./goseg/protocol/conformance/...` then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Runtime structs mix domain configuration and injectable behavior seams, creating high-entropy constructors/normalizers.
evidence: `AccessPointRuntime` holds network/config fields and many function pointers, and `normalizeAccessPointRuntime` assigns defaults for both concerns in one pass.
`wifiRuntime` also combines command execution plumbing with WiFi domain operations and parse/runtime wiring.

`runtime_ops.go` expands the same seam-bag approach into many large operation structs (`RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, `RuntimeStartupOps`), increasing coupling between composition and behavior details.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime types into smaller role-focused components: immutable config structs, behavior interfaces/seams, and dedicated builders per capability; then compose them in thin orchestration entrypoints rather than monolithic normalize/build functions.

findings.summary: Action namespace handling duplicates wrapper APIs instead of using a single namespace-parameterized contract surface.
evidence: `actions.go` defines parallel upload/c2c wrappers (`ParseUploadAction` vs `ParseC2CAction`, `SupportedUploadActions` vs `SupportedC2CActions`, duplicate active/deprecated metadata accessors).
Tests in `actions_test.go` and `contracts_test.go` must repeatedly validate both namespace paths, reflecting API duplication rather than behavior differences.
Adding a new namespace requires extending multiple wrapper families and tests, increasing drift risk.
findings.suggestion: Center callers on one namespace-aware API (parse/supported/contracts/metadata) and keep convenience wrappers generated or minimal; use table-driven tests keyed by namespace to remove mirrored test logic.

findings.summary: Config section updates are implemented as parallel, manually synchronized patterns across structs and patch schema.
evidence: `SysConfig` has multiple nearly identical `Update*Config` methods differing only by section type.
`config_update_schema.go` mirrors section structure via separate patch structs and field registration plumbing, requiring repetitive per-field setup.
Snapshot/update entrypoints in `config.go` add another layer that must stay consistent with section definitions.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a typed section registry (section key -> accessor/mutator/parser metadata) to drive update, patch parse/apply, and snapshots from one source of truth; this reduces per-section boilerplate and synchronization errors.

findings.summary: Tests rely on repeated ad-hoc global seam reset helpers, signaling production APIs that are hard to test in isolated units.
evidence: `resetWifiSeamsForTest` is invoked across many wifi tests and reused by c2c-flow tests.
`resetLeakStateForTest` is maintained and repeatedly called in both leak broadcast and leak handler tests.
`accesspoint_test.go` repeatedly calls global reset helpers (`resetRouterGlobalsForTest`, `resetParameterGlobalsForTest`) before many cases.
findings.suggestion: Move to explicit constructor-injected dependencies and instance-scoped state for these subsystems, then provide shared package test fixtures/builders so tests stop mutating package globals per case.
